title: 06 - Neoplasia - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 6 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck is organized by nomenclature, epidemiology, molecular hallmarks, carcinogens, and clinical diagnosis.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 6: Neoplasia
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis, exam framing, and study-bucket structure.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 6 Study Notes and Exam Prep Questions created from the approved Chapter 6 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress Chapter 6 into single-fact active-recall prompts.

deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 6 occupies book pages 189-242.

cards.front: What does neoplasia literally mean?
cards.back: New growth.

cards.front: What are the two basic components of all tumors?
cards.back: Neoplastic parenchyma and host-derived stroma.

cards.front: What is an adenoma?
cards.back: A benign epithelial neoplasm that forms glands or arises from glandular epithelium.

cards.front: What term is used for malignant epithelial neoplasms?
cards.back: Carcinoma.

cards.front: What term is used for malignant mesenchymal neoplasms?
cards.back: Sarcoma.

cards.front: What is a hamartoma?
cards.back: A disorganized mass of tissue native to the site in which it is found.

cards.front: What is a choristoma?
cards.back: A congenital heterotopic rest of otherwise normal tissue.

cards.front: What does anaplasia mean in pathology?
cards.back: Lack of differentiation; it is a reliable morphologic marker of malignancy.

cards.front: What is dysplasia?
cards.back: Disordered epithelial proliferation with cytologic atypia and loss of normal architectural orientation.

cards.front: What defines carcinoma in situ?
cards.back: Full-thickness epithelial dysplasia with an intact basement membrane and no invasion.

cards.front: What do migration studies suggest about many common cancers?
cards.back: Environmental exposures are major determinants of cancer risk.

cards.front: Why does cancer incidence generally rise with age?
cards.back: Because somatic mutations accumulate over time and immune competence declines.

cards.front: How does chronic inflammation contribute to carcinogenesis?
cards.back: It creates a fertile microenvironment that favors malignant transformation, especially for carcinomas.

cards.front: Immunodeficiency states especially predispose to what broad category of cancers?
cards.back: Virus-induced cancers.

cards.front: What is a precursor lesion?
cards.back: A localized disturbance of epithelial differentiation associated with increased carcinoma risk.

cards.front: Bronchial squamous metaplasia and dysplasia in smokers predispose to what cancer?
cards.back: Lung carcinoma.

cards.front: Villous adenoma of the colon is a precursor lesion for what malignancy?
cards.back: Colorectal carcinoma.

cards.front: Why can inherited cytochrome P-450 variation alter cancer risk?
cards.back: Because it changes how efficiently procarcinogens are converted into active carcinogens.

cards.front: What is a driver mutation?
cards.back: A mutation that confers selective growth or survival advantage and directly contributes to malignant behavior.

cards.front: What is a passenger mutation in cancer?
cards.back: A mutation present in the cancer genome that does not materially drive the malignant phenotype.

cards.front: What is the most commonly mutated oncogene family in human tumors?
cards.back: RAS.

cards.front: What does the BCR-ABL fusion gene encode?
cards.back: A constitutively active tyrosine kinase.

cards.front: Which cell-cycle checkpoint is governed by RB?
cards.back: The G1/S checkpoint.

cards.front: What is p53 often called because of its central role in DNA damage responses?
cards.back: The guardian of the genome.

cards.front: What happens to beta-catenin when APC is lost?
cards.back: It accumulates and translocates to the nucleus, where it activates growth-promoting transcription.

cards.front: What is the Warburg effect?
cards.back: High glucose uptake with aerobic glycolysis that supports synthesis of cellular building blocks for growth.

cards.front: Why are many tumors strongly PET-positive?
cards.back: Because they are glucose hungry and take up large amounts of fluorodeoxyglucose.

cards.front: What is the main oncogenic effect of BCL2 overexpression?
cards.back: Blockade of intrinsic apoptosis and prolonged cell survival.

cards.front: What change gives most cancers replicative immortality?
cards.back: Reactivation or upregulation of telomerase.

cards.front: What must a solid tumor acquire to grow beyond about 1-2 mm?
cards.back: The ability to induce angiogenesis.

cards.front: What is the first major step in local invasion by carcinoma cells?
cards.back: Loosening of tumor-cell contacts, often through reduced E-cadherin function.

cards.front: What happens when tumor PD-L1 engages PD-1 on cytotoxic T cells?
cards.back: The cytotoxic T cells become functionally inhibited and lose effective anti-tumor activity.

cards.front: What is the key difference between direct-acting and indirect-acting carcinogens?
cards.back: Direct-acting carcinogens do not require metabolic activation; indirect-acting carcinogens must be converted to an ultimate carcinogen.

cards.front: What characteristic DNA lesion is caused by ultraviolet radiation?
cards.back: Pyrimidine dimers.

cards.front: Which HTLV-1 protein drives proliferation and genomic instability?
cards.back: Tax.

cards.front: What major tumor suppressor is targeted by high-risk HPV E6?
What major tumor suppressor is targeted by high-risk HPV E7?
cards.back: RB.

cards.front: Which EBV latent membrane protein mimics CD40 signaling and promotes B-cell survival?
cards.back: LMP1.

cards.front: What is the dominant mechanism by which HBV and HCV promote hepatocellular carcinoma?
cards.back: Chronic inflammatory liver injury with regenerative proliferation and reactive oxygen species-mediated DNA damage.

cards.front: Which H. pylori virulence factor is strongly linked to gastric adenocarcinoma?
cards.back: CagA.

cards.front: Which lymphoma is classically associated with H. pylori infection?
cards.back: Gastric MALT lymphoma.

cards.front: What do tumor promoters do after a cell has been initiated by a mutagen?
cards.back: They stimulate proliferation of the initiated cells and enhance tumor development.

cards.front: What mainly causes cancer cachexia?
cards.back: Cytokine-driven metabolic derangement generated by the tumor and host response, not simple nutrient theft by the tumor.

cards.front: Which tumor-produced mediator most commonly causes paraneoplastic hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Parathyroid hormone-related protein (PTHrP).

cards.front: Which is generally more clinically useful in prognosis: tumor grade or tumor stage?
cards.back: Tumor stage.

cards.front: What does TNM stand for in cancer staging?
cards.back: Primary tumor, regional lymph node involvement, and distant metastases.

cards.front: What diagnostic method is routinely used to classify leukemias and lymphomas by surface phenotype?
cards.back: Flow cytometry.

cards.front: What ancillary test helps classify tumors by detecting lineage-specific proteins in tissue sections?
cards.back: Immunohistochemistry.

cards.front: Why are PSA, CEA, and AFP limited as population screening tests?
cards.back: Because they lack the sensitivity and specificity needed for reliable early cancer detection.

cards.front: What is one major post-treatment use of molecular cancer testing?
cards.back: Detection of minimal residual disease.
